passages.explanation: Luke addresses Theophilus again (his Gospel was the 'first book'), summarising that Jesus spent 40 days after his resurrection appearing to the apostles and teaching about the kingdom of God. Jesus tells them not to leave Jerusalem but to wait for the promise of the Father — the baptism of the Holy Spirit. John baptised with water, but they'll be baptised with the Spirit in a few days.

The disciples ask if Jesus is now going to restore Israel's political kingdom. Jesus redirects them: it's not for them to know God's timeline. Instead, they'll receive power when the Holy Spirit comes, and they'll be his witnesses — starting in Jerusalem, expanding to Judea and Samaria, and eventually to the ends of the earth. This verse essentially outlines the entire book of Acts.

Jesus is taken up into heaven while they watch, and a cloud hides him from sight. As they stand staring upward, two men in white (angels) appear and ask why they're looking at the sky. Jesus will come back the same way he left. The message is clear: stop staring and start working.

The apostles return to Jerusalem from the Mount of Olives and go to an upper room. Luke lists the eleven remaining apostles. They devote themselves to prayer together, along with certain women, Mary the mother of Jesus, and his brothers. The same brothers who didn't believe in him during his ministry are now among the believers.

Peter stands up among about 120 believers and addresses the vacancy left by Judas. He quotes two Psalms, interpreting them as prophecies about Judas's betrayal and the need to replace him. Luke includes a parenthetical note about how Judas died — he bought a field with his betrayal money and died gruesomely there, and the place became known as the Field of Blood.

Peter sets the criteria for Judas's replacement: it must be someone who was with them the entire time from John's baptism through the ascension, someone who can serve as a witness to the resurrection. They propose two candidates — Joseph Barsabbas and Matthias. After prayer, they cast lots, and Matthias is chosen to join the eleven. This is the last time lot-casting is used for decision-making in the early church — after the Spirit comes, they'll have a different way of discerning God's will.
